message:
fix: 8 node-lookup paths raise TypeError on unhashable (br-r37-c1-i9whv)

Third follow-up to br-r37-c1-m0io3 / br-r37-c1-g438p. Eight more
node-lookup paths still raised the wrong exception class on
unhashable inputs:

  g.neighbors(unhashable)        fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
  g.adj[unhashable]              fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
  g.nodes[unhashable]            fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
  g[unhashable]                  fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
  g.remove_edge(unhashable, 2)   fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
  g.remove_nodes_from([unhashable])  fnx: silently OK  nx: TypeError
  descendants(g, unhashable)     fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
  ancestors(g, unhashable)       fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError

Drop-in code that does ``except TypeError`` (or
``pytest.raises(TypeError)``) wouldn't trigger — each fnx wrapper
either caught the underlying TypeError and re-raised something
else (KeyError-via-key-preservation, NetworkXError-via-membership
check), or silently iterated past unhashable inputs.

Note: ``is_isolate`` is intentionally NOT in scope. nx's impl
``G.degree(n) == 0`` accepts unhashable list as nbunch (returns
a degree view) and silently returns False — itself a leaky
abstraction in nx — so matching it would be undesirable.

Fix
---
``python/franken_networkx/__init__.py``: add ``hash(node)`` at
the entry of each affected wrapper. Python's ``hash()`` raises
the nx-shaped TypeError for list / set / dict inputs:

- ``_neighbors_with_networkx_missing_node_error``
- ``_remove_edge_with_networkx_missing_edge_error`` (both
  Graph/DiGraph and MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph branches)
- ``_remove_nodes_from_materialized``
- ``_make_keystr_preserving_getitem`` (used by all four
  NodeView types)
- ``AdjacencyView.__getitem__`` and
  ``MultiAdjacencyView.__getitem__``
- ``ancestors`` and ``descendants`` (top-level functions)

``g[n]`` propagates through ``self.adj[n]`` so it picks up the
AdjacencyView fix automatically.

Tests
-----
New ``tests/python/test_unhashable_lookup_paths_parity.py``:
- 8 ``test_*_unhashable_raises_typeerror`` test groups, each
  parametrized over list / set / dict = 24 type-shape cases.
- 5 regression guards on missing-but-hashable inputs (each
  affected wrapper continues to raise its prior exception class
  for missing-node-but-hashable cases).

Full sweep
----------
6910 passed, 0 failed (was 6881 before — +29 new regression cases).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -83,6 +83,7 @@
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-hq5pq","title":"parity: connectivity minimum_st_edge_cut missing","description":"networkx.algorithms.connectivity.minimum_st_edge_cut(G, s, t, flow_func=None, auxiliary=None, residual=None) returns minimum st edge cut. Not exposed by franken_networkx (only minimum_edge_cut and minimum_st_node_cut exist). Add Python wrapper delegating to networkx and export at top-level.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T11:59:43.139262003Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T03:15:05.326488163Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T03:15:05.324637730Z","close_reason":"Already implemented in fnx — top-level export matches nx.connectivity exactly. Landed tests/python/test_connectivity_parity_extras.py (commit 3765516) with 37 cases freezing the contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-hz9k5","title":"parity: drawing wrappers (draw, draw_networkx, draw_networkx_*, display) collapse nx's per-arg signature into generic *args, **kwargs","description":"fnx.draw_networkx, fnx.draw_networkx_nodes, fnx.draw_networkx_edges, fnx.draw_networkx_labels, fnx.draw_networkx_edge_labels, fnx.display all wrap nx via _delegate_draw with generic (G, *args, **kwargs) signature. inspect.signature returns the catch-all instead of nx's documented surface (e.g. nx.draw_networkx_edges has 18 keyword params). Same root cause as approximation namespace fix (functools.wraps). draw also has the variadic-kwarg-name drift (kwargs vs nx's kwds). Fix: apply functools.wraps(nx_func) to each wrapper. Add parity test that asserts inspect.signature(fnx.X) matches inspect.signature(nx.X) for the full drawing namespace.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T05:08:06.159041751Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T05:10:36.083919851Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T05:10:36.082061192Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit c36f08f. Drawing wrappers now use _wrap_nx_drawing helper that applies functools.wraps(nx_func) to each delegator. inspect.signature(fnx.draw_networkx_edges) returns the full 18-parameter nx surface. 18 parity tests in test_drawing_signature_parity.py freeze the contract (signature match + namespace check + metadata).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-i8qr5","title":"is_chordal/complete_to_chordal_graph silently accept K4+self-loop where nx raises","description":"fnx.is_chordal and fnx.complete_to_chordal_graph silently succeed on graphs where nx's internal _is_complete_graph helper raises NetworkXError('Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()').

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(1,2),(1,3),(2,3),(0,0)])  # K4 + self-loop
  >>> fnx.is_chordal(g)                       # True
  >>> nx.is_chordal(nx.Graph(g))
  NetworkXError: Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()

  >>> fnx.complete_to_chordal_graph(g)
  (Graph(nodes=4, edges=7), {0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0})
  >>> nx.complete_to_chordal_graph(nx.Graph(g))
  NetworkXError: Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()

The drift is selective — nx's _is_complete_graph helper is only called when the algorithm reaches a candidate complete subgraph that happens to contain a self-loop. For triangle + self-loop or chain + self-loop, neither algorithm reaches that branch and both nx and fnx return True / a valid result.

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXError) on the K4+self-loop case won't trigger on fnx. fnx's behavior is more permissive but breaks exact-error parity with nx.

Fix: when the input has any self-loop, delegate is_chordal and complete_to_chordal_graph to nx so the exact raise pattern is preserved.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T06:57:04.473475048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-27T07:04:29.309215871Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T07:04:29.305534536Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"br-r37-c1-i9whv","title":"5+ node-lookup paths raise wrong exception on unhashable input","description":"Follow-up to br-r37-c1-m0io3 / -g438p. Several node-lookup paths still raise the wrong exception class on unhashable inputs — drop-in code that does pytest.raises(TypeError) won't trigger:

  g.neighbors([1,2])    fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.adj[[1,2]]          fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.nodes[[1,2]]        fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g[[1,2]]              fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.remove_edge([1,2], 2)  fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.remove_nodes_from([[1,2]])  fnx: silently skips  nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  descendants(g, [1,2])  fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  ancestors(g, [1,2])    fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)

For the dict-getitem cases (adj/nodes/g[n]), nx's underlying dict access raises TypeError because Python's dict tries hash() first. fnx's wrappers catch KeyError-like cases and translate to KeyError, masking the real type issue.

For neighbors/remove_edge/descendants, fnx falls through to a 'node not in graph' check which silently returns False on unhashable, then raises NetworkXError instead of TypeError.

Skip is_isolate (nx itself has a leaky abstraction — accepts unhashable list as nbunch and returns False; matching this is undesirable).

Fix: call hash(node) at the entry of each affected wrapper before any membership check or dict access.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T09:51:28.798377979Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-27T09:52:00.914830260Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-idi4s","title":"Refactor 12 filter/utility funcs out of NX_DELEGATED (hide_*, show_*, no_filter, frozen)","description":"10 filter functions (hide_nodes, hide_edges, hide_diedges, hide_multiedges, hide_multidiedges, show_nodes, show_edges, show_diedges, show_multiedges, show_multidiedges) plus no_filter and frozen all directly import nx and call into nx.classes.filters or nx.classes.function. They classify as NX_DELEGATED.

Per project convention, refactor to use private _xxx_via_nx helpers so the public functions are PY_WRAPPER. Drops NX_DELEGATED count by 12 (40 → 28).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T21:19:04.940688981Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T21:21:29.532368022Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T21:21:29.530228631Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-iss4p","title":"bellman_ford_path_length error message says 'Negative cost cycle' instead of nx's 'Negative cycle'","description":"fnx.bellman_ford_path_length raises NetworkXUnbounded('Negative cost cycle detected.') for negative-cycle inputs. nx uses 'Negative cycle detected.' (no 'cost'). Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXUnbounded, match='Negative cycle') matches nx but NOT fnx.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph()
  >>> for u, v, w in [(1,2,1),(2,3,-3),(3,1,-1)]: g.add_edge(u, v, weight=w)
  >>> fnx.bellman_ford_path_length(g, 1, 3, weight='weight')
  NetworkXUnbounded: Negative cost cycle detected.    # nx: 'Negative cycle detected.'

Sister bead br-r37-c1-wtjho fixed bellman_ford_path. The same defect remains in bellman_ford_path_length: the Rust _raw_bellman_ford_path_length raises with 'Negative cost cycle' wording but the Python wrapper at __init__.py:9879 doesn't catch & re-raise with nx's wording.

Fix: catch NetworkXUnbounded in bellman_ford_path_length and re-raise with 'Negative cycle detected.', mirroring the pattern in bellman_ford_path.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-27T05:21:18.766068801Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-27T05:26:18.957267980Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T05:26:18.954492305Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-iw2hz","title":"12 classic graph generators have adj-iteration order drift from nx (Rust fast paths)","description":"Same pattern as br-r37-c1-o97vk (cycle_graph/wheel_graph): the _rust_* fast paths for many classic graph generators produce adj orderings that don't match nx. The pure-Python construction path for each (used when create_using is set) already matches nx — fix by routing through the Python path always.

Generators that drift (verified via probe):
  * Named (no args): dodecahedral_graph, heawood_graph, frucht_graph,
    sedgewick_maze_graph, truncated_cube_graph,
    truncated_tetrahedron_graph, house_x_graph
  * Parameterised: ladder_graph, circular_ladder_graph,
    circulant_graph(n, offsets), lollipop_graph(m, n),
    barbell_graph(m1, m2)

Repro examples:
  lollipop_graph(3, 4) adj[3]: fnx=[2,4], nx=[4,2]
  barbell_graph(3, 4) adj[3]: fnx=[2,4], nx=[4,2]
  circulant_graph(7,[1,2]) adj[0]: fnx=[1,2,5,6], nx=[6,5,1,2]
  ladder_graph(4) adj[4]: fnx=[0,5], nx=[5,0]
  dodecahedral_graph adj[0]: fnx=[1,10,19], nx=[1,19,10]

Drop-in code that iterated adj[node] of any of these generator-built graphs silently broke (BFS/DFS-strategy tie-breaking, edge-traversal ordering, layouts, visualisations). Fix is the same as the cycle/wheel_graph fix: drop the Rust fast path branch and always use the Python construction.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T14:51:39.196927080Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T14:56:11.250220709Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T14:56:11.248194614Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

@@ -0,0 +1,203 @@
+"""Parity for node-lookup paths on unhashable inputs.
+
+Bead br-r37-c1-i9whv (third follow-up to br-r37-c1-m0io3 / -g438p).
+Eight more node-lookup paths raised the wrong exception class on
+unhashable inputs:
+
+  g.neighbors(unhashable)        fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
+  g.adj[unhashable]              fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
+  g.nodes[unhashable]            fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
+  g[unhashable]                  fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError
+  g.remove_edge(unhashable, 2)   fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
+  g.remove_nodes_from([unhashable])  fnx: silently OK  nx: TypeError
+  descendants(g, unhashable)     fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
+  ancestors(g, unhashable)       fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError
+
+Drop-in code that does ``pytest.raises(TypeError)`` on these
+wouldn't trigger on fnx because each wrapper either caught the
+underlying TypeError and re-raised something else, or silently
+returned False from a membership check that should have raised.
+
+Note: ``is_isolate`` is intentionally NOT in scope — nx's impl
+``G.degree(n) == 0`` accepts unhashable list as nbunch (returning
+a degree view) and silently returns False, which is itself
+arguably an nx leaky abstraction; matching that behavior would
+be undesirable.
+"""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import pytest
+
+import franken_networkx as fnx
+
+try:
+    import networkx as nx
+    HAS_NX = True
+except ImportError:
+    HAS_NX = False
+
+needs_nx = pytest.mark.skipif(not HAS_NX, reason="networkx not installed")
+
+UNHASHABLE = [
+    pytest.param([1, 2], id="list"),
+    pytest.param({1, 2}, id="set"),
+    pytest.param({"a": 1}, id="dict"),
+]
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# neighbors / adj[] / nodes[] / g[]
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_neighbors_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        list(G.neighbors(val))
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        list(GX.neighbors(val))
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_adj_getitem_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        G.adj[val]
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        GX.adj[val]
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_nodes_getitem_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        G.nodes[val]
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        GX.nodes[val]
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_graph_getitem_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        G[val]
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        GX[val]
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# remove_edge / remove_nodes_from
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_remove_edge_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        G.remove_edge(val, 2)
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        GX.remove_edge(val, 2)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_remove_nodes_from_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        G.remove_nodes_from([val])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        GX.remove_nodes_from([val])
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# descendants / ancestors
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_descendants_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.descendants(G, val)
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.descendants(GX, val)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("val", UNHASHABLE)
+def test_ancestors_unhashable_raises_typeerror(val):
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        fnx.ancestors(G, val)
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=r"unhashable type"):
+        nx.ancestors(GX, val)
+
+
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Regression — hashable / missing inputs unaffected
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_neighbors_missing_node_still_raises_networkxerror():
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXError):
+        list(G.neighbors(99))
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXError):
+        list(GX.neighbors(99))
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_adj_getitem_missing_node_still_raises_keyerror():
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(KeyError):
+        G.adj[99]
+    with pytest.raises(KeyError):
+        GX.adj[99]
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_remove_edge_missing_endpoint_still_raises_networkxerror():
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXError):
+        G.remove_edge(99, 1)
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXError):
+        GX.remove_edge(99, 1)
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_remove_nodes_from_missing_hashable_silently_skipped():
+    """Pre-existing parity: remove_nodes_from silently skips missing
+    hashable nodes (per nx contract). The hash-check fix only raises
+    on unhashable inputs; missing-but-hashable inputs continue to be
+    skipped."""
+    G = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.Graph([(1, 2)])
+    G.remove_nodes_from([99, 1])
+    GX.remove_nodes_from([99, 1])
+    assert sorted(G.nodes()) == sorted(GX.nodes()) == [2]
+
+
+@needs_nx
+def test_descendants_missing_node_still_raises_networkxerror():
+    G = fnx.DiGraph([(1, 2)])
+    GX = nx.DiGraph([(1, 2)])
+    with pytest.raises(fnx.NetworkXError):
+        fnx.descendants(G, 99)
+    with pytest.raises(nx.NetworkXError):
+        nx.descendants(GX, 99)
